chunk_summary: Visible severe wasting is a high-sensitivity bedside screen for severe acute malnutrition (SAM) in under-five children, used when anthropometric measurements are unavailable or impractical.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical signs of visible severe wasting and its role in SAM screening.
Learn the bedside assessment techniques for identifying visible severe wasting in children under five.
Recognize false positives and negatives in visible severe wasting screening and their implications.
Integrate visible severe wasting screening with other SAM diagnostic tools like MUAC and oedema checks.
key_facts: Visible severe wasting is a high-sensitivity sign used in IMCI/IMNCI protocols to prevent missed SAM cases.
Assessment involves observing loss of subcutaneous fat, prominent ribs/scapulae, thin limbs, and 'baggy pants' skin folds.
False positives include constitutionally thin children with preserved appetite and stable growth trajectory.
False negatives occur in cases of oedema, which can mask low tissue mass; bilateral pitting oedema checks are essential.
Disposition rule: Treat visible severe wasting as SAM until proven otherwise; initiate urgent danger sign assessment and referral.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Visible severe wasting is a high-sensitivity sign used in IMCI/IMNCI logic to prevent missed severe acute malnutrition when length/height-based indices cannot be obtained immediately (IMNCI Chart Booklet for Medical Officers, 2023).
document_key_topics: Visible severe wasting as a SAM screening tool
False positives and negatives in SAM diagnosis
Integration of visible wasting with anthropometric measures
